perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. It will not constitute a violation of this Agreement for GSEnergy to disclose Information as required by a governmental body or a court of competent jurisdiction or as otherwise required by law, provided that the Company has been given notice of such requirement and been afforded a reasonable opportunity to contest it, if applicable. 6. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the choice of law provision further down in the document and inserting an exception before it could lead to confusion about which state's laws govern the contract, particularly if the exception refers to laws outside of Massachusetts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law (M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301(a)) recognizes the parties’ ability to choose the law that governs their agreement. However, the delayed placement can cause doubt, and the added clause regarding legal disclosure might be interpreted as prioritizing other jurisdictions' laws under certain conditions. This could introduce ambiguity and undermine the enforceability of the chosen law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-301 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1-301 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-301: Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1–301. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2–402; (2) sections 2A–105 and 2A–106; (3) section 4–102; (4) section 4A–507; (5) section 5–116; (6) section 8–110; and (7) sections 9–301 through 9–307. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The relocation and insertion of an exception before the choice of law provision creates a structural flaw that introduces ambiguity and potential conflict. M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301(a), as evidenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows parties to choose the governing law. However, the changed text undermines the clarity and enforceability of this choice by potentially subjecting the agreement to other jurisdictions' laws. The law snippet explicitly states that parties can agree on the governing law ('the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern'). By adding an exception related to governmental or court-ordered disclosures, and placing it before the choice of law provision, the modified contract creates a legal contradiction. This structural flaw increases the likelihood that other laws might supersede Massachusetts law, thus contradicting the original intent of M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301(a). This ambiguity constitutes a 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' as it obscures the primary choice of law.
